content:
# Genesis Chapter 8 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 5r–5v | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_008_page_001.jpg, GEN_008_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0009

---

**1** And God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the animals that were with him in the ark. And God caused a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters subsided.

**2** The fountains of the deep and the windows of the heavens were closed, and the rain from the heavens was restrained.

**3** And the waters receded from upon the earth, going and receding. And at the end of one hundred and fifty days the waters diminished.

**4** And the ark came to rest in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat.

**5** And the waters continued to diminish until the tenth month. In the tenth month, on the first day of the month, the tops of the mountains appeared.

**6** And it came to pass at the end of forty days that Noah opened the window of the ark that he had made.

**7** And he sent out the raven, and it went back and forth until the waters were dried up from upon the earth.

**8** And he sent out the dove from with him, to see whether the waters had subsided from the face of the ground.

**9** But the dove found no resting place for the sole of her foot, and she returned to him into the ark, for the waters were upon the face of all the earth. And he stretched out his hand and took her and brought her in to him into the ark.

**10** And he waited still another seven days, and again he sent out the dove from the ark.

**11** And the dove came to him at evening, and behold — in her mouth was a freshly plucked olive leaf. So Noah knew that the waters had subsided from upon the earth.

**12** And he waited still another seven days, and sent out the dove; and she did not return to him again.

**13** And it came to pass in the six-hundred-and-first year, in the first month, on the first day of the month, the waters were dried from upon the earth. And Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked, and behold — the face of the ground was dry.

**14** And in the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the earth was dry.

**15** And God spoke to Noah, saying,

**16** "Go out from the ark — you, and your wife, and your sons, and your sons' wives with you.

**17** Every living thing that is with you of all flesh — the birds and the beasts and every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth — bring out with you, that they may teem upon the earth and be fruitful and multiply upon the earth."

**18** And Noah went out, and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives with him.

**19** Every beast, every creeping thing, and every bird — everything that moves upon the earth — went out from the ark by their families.

**20** And Noah built an altar to the LORD, and took from every clean animal and from every clean bird and offered burnt offerings upon the altar.

**21** And the LORD smelled the pleasing aroma, and the LORD said in His heart, "I will not again curse the ground on account of the human beings, for the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth. And I will not again strike down every living thing as I have done.

**22** While the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night shall not cease."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of three manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 8 spans Folios 5r–5v. Folio 5r (page 001 of this chapter) is the same folio that carries the latter portion of Genesis 7 in its upper sections; the Genesis 8 text begins toward the foot of that folio and continues into Folio 5v. Folio 5v (page 002 of this chapter) contains the main body of Genesis 8 through verse 22 and into the opening of Genesis 9. The three-column manuscript layout is clear on both folios. The Tiberian vocalization — qamets, patah, tsere, hiriq, shureq, and the full system of cantillation marks (te'amim) — is present and legible throughout. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) appears in verses 20, 21 (twice) with the standard Masoretic qere notation. The divine name אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) appears in verses 1 and 15. The paragraph (parashah) division markers are visible at the major literary seams, including the major section break before verse 20 (the altar and offering), and the concluding divine speech of verses 21–22.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 8 was read directly from the folio (image labeled GEN_008_vaticanus_page_001.jpg; catalogue reference Vat.gr.1209/0009). The folio image carries the chapter number annotation "CAP. VIII" in the right margin, confirming that Genesis 8 appears on this folio following Genesis 7. The Greek text is written in the characteristic three-column uncial hand of the Vaticanus. The LXX Genesis 8 preserves the narrative sequence of the MT closely but presents several lexical and textual divergences documented in full below, most notably in the opening of verse 1 (the wind/ruach over the waters), the raven and dove sequence (vv. 6–12), and the divine speech of verses 21–22.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת נֹחַ (vayyizkor Elohim et Noach) — "And God remembered Noah" (v. 1)**

The opening word of Genesis 8 is וַיִּזְכֹּר — "and He remembered." The verb זָכַר (zakar) in Hebrew carries far more weight than mere mental recall. In its full biblical sense, divine remembering is an active, covenantal, relational act: to remember, in the idiom of the Hebrew Bible, is to turn toward with intention, to act on behalf of, to honor a covenantal bond by bringing it to active expression. When God remembers Noah, the text is not saying that God had momentarily forgotten him; it is saying that the moment of covenantal action has arrived.

The verb זָכַר in the context of divine action is consistently covenantal throughout the Hebrew Bible:
- Genesis 19:29: "And it came to pass, when God destroyed the cities of the plain, that God remembered Abraham" — and saved Lot on account of Abraham.
- Exodus 2:24: "And God heard their groaning, and God remembered His covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob" — and the exodus began.
- Exodus 6:5: "And I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel... and I have remembered My covenant."
- Psalm 105:8, 106:45: God remembers His covenant as the ground of His redemptive action.

In each case, divine remembering is not a cognitive event but a covenantal turning: God's attention is actively oriented toward the object of His remembrance, and what follows is saving action. In Genesis 8:1, the object of the divine remembering is Noah — and all the living things with him in the ark. The remembering encompasses all the creatures that God had commanded to enter the ark; the covenantal commitment extends through Noah to the entire ark-company, as is appropriate for the man who is the covenant partner on behalf of all creation.

The opening of Genesis 8 with divine remembering thus functions as the theological hinge of the entire flood narrative. Genesis 6 established the problem (human corruption, divine grief, the decision to blot out). Genesis 7 executed the judgment (the waters rose, all land life perished). Genesis 8:1 opens the resolution: "And God remembered Noah." The same God who had grieved over creation (6:6) and shut Noah in the ark (7:16) now acts in covenantal remembrance to reverse the flood. The arc of the flood narrative is not judgment-only but judgment-and-remembrance: the flood is bounded by grief on one end and remembrance on the other.

The formal translation renders וַיִּזְכֹּר as "remembered" — the most formally faithful rendering, following KJV, ESV, NASB, JPS. This preserves the full covenantal resonance of the Hebrew word without substituting a weaker English equivalent ("thought of," "turned his attention to"). "Remembered" is the word, and it carries all of this weight.

**The Echo of Genesis 1:2 — The Ruach over the Waters**

The second clause of verse 1 is: וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ עַל הָאָרֶץ — "And God caused a wind (ruach) to pass over the earth." The word רוּחַ (ruach) means wind, breath, spirit — it is the same capacious, polyvalent term that appears in the most celebrated use in the entire Hebrew Bible: Genesis 1:2, where the ruach Elohim (spirit/wind of God) hovers over the face of the waters at the creation.

The connection is not incidental. Genesis 8:1 is one of the most carefully crafted echoes in the entire flood narrative. The structural parallel between the two verses is exact and deliberate:

| Genesis 1:2 | Genesis 8:1 |
|---|---|
| וְרוּחַ אֱלֹהִים מְרַחֶפֶת עַל פְּנֵי הַמָּיִם ("and the ruach of God was hovering over the face of the waters") | וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ עַל הָאָרֶץ ("and God caused a ruach to pass over the earth") |
| The ruach moves over the primordial waters | The ruach moves over the flood waters |
| Creation begins | Re-creation begins |
| The deep (tehom) is present and unformed | The deep (tehom) is present and subsiding |

In Genesis 1:2, the Spirit/wind of God moving over the waters is the precondition for the creative word that follows — light, firmament, dry land. In Genesis 8:1, the wind of God moving over the flood waters is the beginning of the same sequence in reverse: the waters that covered the earth in de-creation now begin to subside as God's wind acts upon them, and dry land will re-emerge just as it did in the original creation. The flood narrative is structured as a de-creation followed by a re-creation, and the ruach over the waters is the literary and theological signal of the turn from one to the other.

The formal translation renders רוּחַ as "a wind" in verse 1 — capturing the concrete meteorological reality of a drying wind while leaving the deeper resonance with Genesis 1:2 available to the attentive reader. The text does not call it "the Spirit of God" here (it is simply רוּחַ, without the construct "of God" as in 1:2), but the echo is present in the structure of the narrative and in the act of God causing this wind to pass over the waters. The formal translation follows the Hebrew: "God caused a wind to pass over the earth."

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this: καὶ ἐπήγαγεν ὁ θεὸς πνεῦμα ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ("and God brought a spirit/wind upon the earth") — using πνεῦμα (pneuma, "spirit/wind"), the standard LXX rendering for רוּחַ, and the verb ἐπάγω ("to bring upon, to lead upon"). The LXX rendering is slightly more forceful than "pass over" — the wind is "brought upon" the earth — but the semantic range is equivalent.

**2. הַר אֲרָרָט (har Ararat) — "mountains of Ararat" (v. 4)**

The text states that the ark rested עַל הָרֵי אֲרָרָט — "upon the mountains of Ararat." The form הָרֵי is the construct plural of הַר (har, "mountain") — "mountains of Ararat," not "the mountain of Ararat." This is the exact form of the Hebrew, and it is significant: the text does not name a single peak but a mountain range. The proper noun אֲרָרָט (Ararat) refers to the ancient kingdom and region of Urartu, known from Assyrian records, which corresponds to the mountainous region of modern eastern Turkey, Armenia, and northwestern Iran, centered on the area around Lake Van.

The tradition of identifying the "mount Ararat" of Genesis 8:4 with the specific volcanic peak known in modern Turkish as Büyük Ağrı Dağı (Greater Ararat, elevation 5,137m) is ancient but is not what the Hebrew text says. The Hebrew plural הָרֵי אֲרָרָט means "the mountains of the Ararat region" — the ark came to rest somewhere in that highland range, not on a specific named peak. This is a case where the singular English tradition ("Mount Ararat") reflects an interpretive tradition rather than the Hebrew text.

The formal translation renders: "upon the mountains of Ararat," following the Hebrew plural exactly. This is the rendering of KJV ("mountains of Ararat"), ESV ("mountains of Ararat"), NASB ("mountains of Ararat"), and JPS ("mountains of Ararat"). The NLT and several dynamic translations render "Mount Ararat" — a simplification that introduces a specificity the Hebrew does not claim.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: ἐπὶ τὰ ὄρη τὰ Αραρατ — "upon the mountains of Ararat" — using the Greek plural ὄρη (ore, "mountains"), matching the MT's plural exactly. The LXX text confirms the plural reading.

**3. The Flood Calendar — Chronological Structure of Genesis 8**

Genesis 8 is rich with precise calendar dates, and their function is to ground the flood narrative in the structure of measured time. The dates given in the chapter, in sequence, are:

| Reference | Date | Event |
|---|---|---|
| 7:11 | Year 600, Month 2, Day 17 | Flood begins |
| 7:24 | (+150 days) | Waters prevail |
| 8:3–4 | Year 600, Month 7, Day 17 | Waters diminish; ark rests on Ararat |
| 8:5 | Year 600, Month 10, Day 1 | Mountain tops appear |
| 8:6 | (+40 days from 10/1) | Noah opens window |
| 8:10 | (+7 days) | First dove sent again |
| 8:12 | (+7 days) | Second dove sent again; does not return |
| 8:13 | Year 601, Month 1, Day 1 | Earth's surface dries |
| 8:14 | Year 601, Month 2, Day 27 | Earth fully dry |

The ark rested on Ararat on the seventeenth day of the seventh month — exactly 150 days after the flood began on the seventeenth day of the second month (7:11). Five months of thirty days each = 150 days. This internal mathematical consistency is characteristic of the P source's precise calendrical framework.

The gap between the ground becoming visibly dry (1/1 of Year 601) and the earth being fully dry (2/27 of Year 601) — a period of fifty-seven days — may reflect a distinction between the surface appearing dry and the ground being structurally dry enough to support the weight of people and animals exiting the ark.

The total duration of the flood from first rain (Month 2, Day 17 of Year 600) to the earth being fully dry (Month 2, Day 27 of Year 601) is approximately 370 days — one full solar year plus 10 days. This has been noted in both ancient and modern scholarship: the flood narrative spans exactly one year (in the sense of encompassing two occurrences of the same calendar date, Month 2, Day 17) and then ten additional days until the full drying.

The formal translation renders all dates literally following the MT: "seventh month," "seventeenth day," "tenth month," etc. No calendar system is identified by name because the text does not identify one.

**4. הָעֹרֵב / הַיּוֹנָה (ha-orev / ha-yonah) — The Raven and the Dove (vv. 6–12)**

The raven-and-dove sequence of verses 6–12 is one of the most carefully constructed literary passages in the flood narrative. The two birds perform contrasting literary and theological roles:

**The Raven (v. 7):**

וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת הָעֹרֵב — "And he sent out the raven." The raven (עֹרֵב, orev) is sent first. The verb that describes its subsequent behavior is וַיֵּצֵא יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב — literally "and it went out, going and returning" — or "it went back and forth." The raven's behavior is described with the infinitive absolute (יָצוֹא) paired with the finite verb (וַיֵּצֵא) and the companion verb שׁוּב ("to return"), producing the iterative sense: the raven went back and forth repeatedly — it kept going and coming back — "until the waters were dried up from upon the earth."

The raven does not return to Noah. It does not bring a message. It simply flies back and forth between the ark and the land until the earth is dry. The text does not moralize about the raven. It was sent; it went; it did what ravens do. The raven is a scavenger bird, a carrion-feeder, and the flood waters would have left abundant floating carrion for it to land on; it had no need to return to the ark. The narrative does not condemn the raven, but its failure to serve as a messenger or to return with information sets up the contrast with the dove.

**The Dove (vv. 8–12):**

The dove is sent three times, and the three sendings are structurally parallel with escalating results:

- First sending: The dove finds no resting place (מָנוֹחַ, manoach — "a place of rest/alighting") for the sole of her foot, and returns. Noah extends his hand and takes her in.
- Second sending (after seven days): The dove returns at evening with a freshly plucked olive leaf in her mouth.
- Third sending (after another seven days): The dove does not return.

The dove's three stages — no sign, olive leaf, no return — map the transition from waters covering everything, to waters receding enough for olive trees to stand above water, to the earth being dry enough for a bird to live independently of the ark. The dove functions as a living barometer and herald of the new world.

The word מָנוֹחַ (manoach, "resting place") in verse 9 echoes the name of Noah's father Lamech in Genesis 5:29: "This one will bring us relief (manoach/nuach)." The root נוּחַ (nuach, "to rest") underlies the name Noah (נֹחַ), the ark's resting (נָחָה) on Ararat (v. 4), and the dove's failure to find a resting place (מָנוֹחַ). The entire narrative is saturated with the nuach/Noah wordplay: the ark rests (8:4, root nuach), the dove seeks rest (manoach), Noah receives rest and brings rest, and the pleasing aroma (reyach nichoach, v. 21) will complete the wordplay on Noah's name at the chapter's climax.

The olive leaf is עָלֵה זַיִת טָרָף — "a freshly plucked olive leaf." The word טָרָף (taraph) means "plucked, torn off" — it is the same root used for torn prey (a predator "tears" its food). The leaf was freshly torn from a living branch. This is not a dead leaf floating on the water; it is a living leaf from a living olive tree — proof that land and vegetation have returned.

The formal translation renders the first dove-sending's description: "no resting place for the sole of her foot" — preserving the anatomical specificity of the Hebrew כַּף רַגְלָהּ (kaph raglab, "the sole/palm of her foot"). The image is exact and tactile: the dove could not find solid ground for the bottom of her foot — not just "land" but the specific experience of a bird trying to set down and finding nothing firm enough to bear her. The formal translation renders the olive leaf as "freshly plucked" (τάρφ / taraph) following the MT's specific word.

**5. עֲלֵה זַיִת טָרָף (aleh zayit taraph) — "freshly plucked olive leaf" (v. 11)**

The Hebrew עָלֵה זַיִת טָרָף — an olive leaf, freshly plucked/torn — uses the word טָרָף (taraph) which carries the weight of freshness and life: the leaf had been torn directly from a living branch. This is the first mention of the olive tree in the Bible, and the detail is theologically significant: olive trees are among the most deep-rooted, long-lived, and drought/flood-resistant of all trees in the ancient Near East. An olive tree surviving the flood, growing visibly above the receding waters, is the herald of renewed vegetation and renewed land. The olive will become, in the biblical and subsequent Mediterranean tradition, the tree of peace and of God's anointing — the first post-flood plant is the olive, and it is introduced here as a living green leaf torn from a branch.

The formal translation renders: "a freshly plucked olive leaf." The word "freshly" captures the טָרָף sense of recent tearing — not a dried or dead leaf but something alive. Several translations render "a freshly plucked olive leaf" (ESV, NASB), "an olive leaf" (NIV, KJV), or "a plucked olive leaf" (JPS). The formal translation follows "freshly plucked" as the most faithful rendering of the participle.

**6. וַיָּרַח יְהוָה אֶת רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ — The Pleasing Aroma and the Noah Wordplay (v. 21)**

Verse 21 opens with one of the most significant clauses in the flood narrative: וַיָּרַח יְהוָה אֶת רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ — "And the LORD smelled the pleasing aroma."

The word רֵיחַ (reyach) means "aroma, smell, scent." The word נִיחֹחַ (nichoach) is a participle/noun from the root נוּחַ (nuach, "to rest, to be at rest, to be soothing") — it means something that settles, soothes, or brings rest. Together, רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (reyach nichoach) is the technical term for the "pleasing/soothing aroma" of a sacrifice — the aroma that is acceptable to God, that settles and satisfies rather than offending. The phrase occurs throughout the Levitical legislation as the standard description of an acceptable burnt offering: it is the sensory register in which the divine-human covenantal transaction of sacrifice is consummated.

But the word נִיחֹחַ is unmistakably, inseparably rooted in the same root as the name נֹחַ (Noah). The reyach nichoach — the pleasing, rest-bringing aroma of Noah's altar — is the aroma of Noah. Lamech prophesied in 5:29 that Noah (nuach/nacham) would bring relief/rest. The flood narrative has enacted that promise in the negative (rest from the cursed ground through judgment). Now, at the altar, the positive fulfillment arrives: Noah's offering produces a nichoach aroma — a Noah-scent, a rest-scent — that rises to the LORD, and the LORD's response is the unconditional promise of verses 21–22.

The formal translation renders: "the pleasing aroma." The word "pleasing" is the standard formal equivalence rendering of נִיחֹחַ (KJV, ESV, NASB, JPS all render "pleasing aroma"). "Sweet savour" (KJV older editions) is archaic. "Soothing aroma" (NASB) captures the "bringing to rest" sense of the root more precisely. The formal translation follows "pleasing aroma" as the most widely established formal rendering, while noting the nuach/Noah wordplay here rather than attempting to render it in English.

**7. יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו — "The inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth" (v. 21) — Same Diagnosis, Opposite Conclusion**

The most theologically striking feature of verse 21 is its near-verbatim repetition of the diagnostic statement of Genesis 6:5, but in a reversed theological context:

| Genesis 6:5 | Genesis 8:21 |
|---|---|
| כִּי רַע יֵצֶר מַחְשְׁבֹת לִבּוֹ רַק רַע כָּל הַיּוֹם ("for the inclination of his heart is only evil all the day") | כִּי יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו ("for the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth") |
| **Conclusion:** The LORD decides to send the flood — to blot out all flesh | **Conclusion:** The LORD decides never to send the flood again |

The same diagnosis — human יֵצֶר (yetser, inclination) is evil — produces in Genesis 6:5 the decision to destroy and in Genesis 8:21 the decision to preserve. The flood did not fix the human problem. The diagnosis of 6:5 stands unrevised. But the conclusion has been radically reversed: because the human heart is evil from youth, God will not again destroy the world. The mercy of 8:21 is not based on improved humanity but on divine resolution — God has looked at the same broken creature and decided on a different response.

This reversal is one of the most theologically profound moments in the entire Pentateuch. It carries several possible interpretive implications:

- The flood was not meant to solve the human problem by destroying evil humanity and starting over with good humanity (Noah is not described as morally superior in 8:21 — the diagnosis encompasses all humanity including Noah and his descendants). If the problem cannot be solved by destruction, destruction will not be the method again.
- The divine decision of 8:21 is an act of pure grace — not earned by human improvement, not conditioned on human performance, but given because God has chosen to bear with the incurably broken creature rather than destroy it.
- The 8:21 clause adds a specification absent from 6:5: מִנְּעֻרָיו — "from his youth." The evil inclination is not an acquired corruption but a constitutional one, present from youth (נְעוּרִים, neorim — youth, early life). This is one of the earliest biblical affirmations of what later theology will call original sin or radical human depravity: the disorder of the human heart is not circumstantial but congenital, not formed by environment but present from youth.

The formal translation renders יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם as "the inclination of the human heart" — following the established rendering of יֵצֶר as "inclination" (see note at Genesis 6:5). The word מִנְּעֻרָיו is literally "from his youth" — the formal translation renders it "from his youth," preserving the Hebrew's specific developmental language.

**8. אַקַּלֵּל — "I will not again curse the ground" (v. 21)**

The first part of the divine promise in verse 21 reads: לֹא אֹסִף לְקַלֵּל עוֹד אֶת הָאֲדָמָה — "I will not again curse the ground." The verb קָלַל (qalal, Piel: קַלֵּל) means "to curse, to make light/slight, to treat as contemptible, to declare accursed." The ground (אֲדָמָה, adamah) was cursed in Genesis 3:17 — the curse of thorns and thistles and painful toil — and the flood continued and intensified that curse to its logical end (the ground was covered by water, all life on it destroyed). The divine promise here is not that the 3:17 curse is lifted (it is not — thorns and thistles remain, toil continues), but that the ground will not again be subject to the catastrophic curse of total flooding.

The phrase עַל בַּעֲבוּר הָאָדָם — "on account of the human beings" — gives the ground of the promise: the LORD will not again curse the ground on account of the same human beings whose evil caused the flood in the first place. The promise is thus not based on human merit but on divine restraint — a resolve not to repeat the judgment.

The second part: וְלֹא אֹסִף עוֹד לְהַכּוֹת אֶת כָּל חַי — "and I will not again strike down every living thing as I have done." The verb נָכָה (nakah, Hiphil: לְהַכּוֹת) means "to strike, to smite, to kill." The promise encompasses not just the ground but all living things: God will not again perform the mass extinction of the flood.

**9. עֹד כָּל יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ — "while the earth remains" (v. 22)**

Verse 22 is the closing promise: "While the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night shall not cease." The phrase עֹד כָּל יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ — "still all the days of the earth," or idiomatically "as long as the earth endures" — frames the promise within a temporal limit: as long as this earth lasts. The promise is made for the duration of the present created order, not unconditionally beyond it. The pairing of opposing seasons and times — seedtime/harvest, cold/heat, summer/winter, day/night — is a comprehensive enumeration of the rhythms of the natural order, covering agricultural cycle, temperature cycle, seasonal cycle, and diurnal cycle. All of these will persist without interruption.

The literary pattern of the list is three pairs of opposites, each pair expressing a complete cycle through its two poles. The list is a formal affirmation of cosmic regularity: the flood has not destabilized the created rhythms of time and season. God's promise guarantees the continuation of the created order against any future catastrophic interruption.

The formal translation renders: "shall not cease" for the verb שָׁבַת (shavat) — the same root as Sabbath (שַׁבָּת, shabbat). The day and night, the seasons, the seedtime and harvest: these will not observe their own sabbath — they will not cease, rest, or stop. The use of the Sabbath-root here is almost certainly deliberate: the cessation that would be catastrophic for the created order (the seasons stopping) is precisely what the flood seemed to threaten, and the promise is that this will never happen again.

**10. Noah's Altar — The First Altar in the Bible (v. 20)**

Verse 20 records the first construction of an altar in the biblical narrative: וַיִּבֶן נֹחַ מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה — "And Noah built an altar to the LORD." The word מִזְבֵּחַ (mizbeach) comes from the root זָבַח (zavach, "to slaughter for sacrifice") — it is literally "a place of slaughter," an altar for sacrifice. This is the first occurrence of the word מִזְבֵּחַ in the Bible. Previous worship — Abel's offering, Cain's offering (Genesis 4:3–4) — was described without mentioning an altar. Noah's construction of an altar is the first formal act of worship through constructed sacred space in the biblical narrative.

The offering: Noah takes from "every clean animal and every clean bird" — the categories that had been brought aboard in extra pairs (seven pairs of clean animals, Genesis 7:2–3) precisely to make this kind of offering possible. The excess of clean animals over unclean was divinely planned provision for the first post-flood sacrifice. The seven pairs were not only about genetic viability of clean species; they anticipated the altar.

The technical term עֹלוֹת (olot, "burnt offerings") refers to the specific sacrifice in which the entire animal is consumed on the altar — wholly burned, entirely given to God with nothing held back. The olah is the sacrifice of total consecration. Noah's first act after emerging from the ark is to give everything back to God — the animals he had preserved become the offering that consecrates the new world.

**11. Divine Name Pattern in Genesis 8**

As in Genesis 6–7, Genesis 8 uses both divine names in a discernible pattern:

- אֱלֹהִים (God): verses 1, 15 — the cosmological/Priestly sections (God remembers, God speaks the exit command)
- יְהוָה (the LORD): verses 20, 21 (twice) — the altar and the promise

The pattern in chapter 8 is the structural inverse of Genesis 7: where chapter 7 opened with YHWH's intimate command and closed with Elohim's architectural work, chapter 8 opens with Elohim's cosmic act of remembrance and closes with YHWH's intimate promise at the altar. The personal name of God appears at the moments of intimate covenantal exchange — the offering and the promise — while the universal name appears at the moments of cosmic action (the wind over the waters, the exit command).

The formal translation maintains: "the LORD" for יְהוָה and "God" for אֱלֹהִים, with no harmonization, exactly as in the established pattern of Genesis 1–7.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 8

The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 8 was read directly. The following variants and divergences from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8:1 | וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ עַל הָאָרֶץ ("and God caused a wind to pass over the earth") | καὶ ἐπήγαγεν ὁ θεὸς πνεῦμα ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ("and God brought a spirit/wind upon the earth") | "God caused a wind to pass over the earth" | LXX ἐπάγω ("bring upon") vs. MT עָבַר ("pass over"); LXX slightly more forceful; MT followed |
| 8:4 | הָרֵי אֲרָרָט ("mountains of Ararat") | ὄρη τὰ Αραρατ ("mountains of Ararat") | "mountains of Ararat" | LXX confirms MT plural ὄρη |
| 8:7 | וַיֵּצֵא יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב ("and it went out, going and returning") | ὃ ἐξελθὸν οὐχ ὑπέστρεψεν ("which going out did not return") | "it went back and forth" | LXX reads as raven not returning at all; MT's iterative idiom (going and returning) rendered as repeated motion until waters dry up; MT followed |
| 8:11 | עָלֵה זַיִת טָרָף ("a freshly plucked olive leaf") | φύλλον ἐλαίας κάρφος ("a sprig/twig of olive leaf") | "a freshly plucked olive leaf" | LXX uses κάρφος ("twig, small piece") rather than translating τάρφ ("freshly torn/plucked"); MT's specific word for fresh tearing followed |
| 8:13 | וַיָּסַר נֹחַ אֶת מִכְסֵה הַתֵּבָה ("and Noah removed the covering of the ark") | καὶ ἀπεκάλυψεν Νωε τὴν στέγην τῆς κιβωτοῦ ("and Noah uncovered the roof of the ark") | "Noah removed the covering of the ark" | LXX στέγη ("roof") specifies what מִכְסֵה ("covering") refers to; MT's general "covering" retained |
| 8:21 | יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו ("the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth") | ἡ διάνοια τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐπιμελῶς ἐπίκειται ἐπὶ τὰ πονηρὰ ἐκ νεότητος ("the mind/thought of man is earnestly bent toward evil things from youth") | "the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth" | LXX renders יֵצֶר with διάνοια ("thought, mind, understanding") and adds ἐπιμελῶς ("earnestly, carefully") absent from MT; MT's יֵצֶר ("inclination") retained |
| 8:21 | לֹא אֹסִף לְקַלֵּל עוֹד אֶת הָאֲדָמָה ("I will not again curse the ground") | οὐ προσθήσω ἔτι τοῦ καταράσασθαι τὴν γῆν ("I will not add again to curse the earth") | "I will not again curse the ground" | LXX τὴν γῆν ("the earth/land") broadly vs. MT's specific הָאֲדָמָה ("the ground/soil") — MT's specific term retained |
| 8:22 | עֹד כָּל יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ ("while all the days of the earth remain") | πάσας τὰς ἡμέρας τῆς γῆς ("all the days of the earth") | "while the earth remains" | LXX matches MT closely; English idiom "while the earth remains" renders the Hebrew temporal phrase |

**Notable LXX divergence — 8:7 (The Raven):**

The MT's description of the raven — וַיֵּצֵא יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב — uses the infinitive absolute construction (yatso vashot: "going and returning") to express iterative, back-and-forth motion "until the waters were dried up." The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this as ὃ ἐξελθὸν οὐχ ὑπέστρεψεν — "which going out did not return" — reading the verse as the raven simply not coming back, rather than circling back and forth. The LXX reading makes the raven more clearly a failed messenger (it does not return, so Noah gets no information), while the MT's iterative reading describes the raven as repeatedly going out and coming back until the earth dries — a different behavioral picture.

The formal translation follows the MT's iterative reading: "it went back and forth until the waters were dried up from upon the earth." This rendering captures the Hebrew infinitive absolute construction and preserves the distinction between the raven (which circles without bringing information) and the dove (which returns with the olive leaf). The LXX's reading, while coherent, flattens the behavioral distinction between the two birds.

---

### DSS Note — Genesis 8

The Dead Sea Scrolls contain fragmentary evidence relevant to Genesis 8:

- **4QGenb (4Q2)**: Fragmentary Genesis manuscript from Qumran Cave 4. Preserves portions of the early Genesis narrative. The fragments in the Genesis 8 range are limited; where they survive, they agree substantially with the MT as preserved in the Leningrad Codex without significant textual variants.
- **4QGene (4Q5)**: Another fragmentary Genesis manuscript from Cave 4. The fragments that touch the Genesis 8 range are minimal and do not contradict the MT.
- **1QapGen (1Q20, the Genesis Apocryphon)**: The Aramaic Genesis Apocryphon includes an elaborate retelling of the Noah flood narrative in columns VI–XVII. The Apocryphon's retelling of the raven and dove sequence (corresponding to Genesis 8:6–12) expands the passage considerably in the first-person voice of Noah, with emotional descriptions of Noah's anxiety and his relief at the dove's return with the olive leaf. The Apocryphon's description of the pleasing aroma offering (corresponding to Genesis 8:20–21) is also expanded, with Noah describing the preparation of the sacrifice and the divine acceptance in more detail. The Apocryphon follows the MT narrative sequence throughout but adds extensive first-person reflection.
- **Jubilees (4Q216–224)**: The book of Jubilees retells the Genesis 8 flood calendar in the framework of its 364-day solar calendar, placing the events of the flood on specific days that are significant in the Jubilees festival calendar. In particular, Jubilees dates Noah's exit from the ark and the building of the altar (corresponding to Genesis 8:18–20) to the first day of the third month — the date of the feast of Shavuot/Weeks in the Jubilees calendar — interpreting Noah's covenant as the precedent for the Sinai covenant celebrated on the same feast day. This interpretation demonstrates that Genesis 8:20–22 was read as a covenantal transaction of the same order as Sinai in Second Temple Judaism.

### Structural and Theological Note

Genesis 8 functions as the turning chapter of the flood narrative — the chapter of reversal, re-creation, and divine commitment. Its structure mirrors and inverts the structure of the flood onset in Genesis 7.

**1. The De-creation Reversed (vv. 1–5)**

Genesis 7 described the systematic unmaking of the creation order: the fountains of the deep burst open, the windows of heaven were opened, the waters rose and covered everything. Genesis 8 describes the systematic restoration of the creation order, using the same vocabulary in reverse:

| Genesis 7 (De-creation) | Genesis 8 (Re-creation) |
|---|---|
| 7:11 — fountains of the deep burst open | 8:2 — fountains of the deep were closed |
| 7:11 — windows of heaven were opened | 8:2 — windows of heaven were closed |
| 7:19–20 — mountains covered, dry land gone | 8:5 — mountain tops appeared |
| 7:24 — waters prevailed 150 days | 8:3 — waters diminished after 150 days |

The symmetry is exact and structural. The narrative is formally chiastic around the pivot of 8:1 — "And God remembered Noah." The de-creation section of the flood narrative (7:11–24) is balanced by the re-creation section (8:1–14), and the pivot is the act of divine remembering that sets the reversal in motion.

**2. The Re-creation Vocabulary — Genesis 1 Echoes in Genesis 8**

Several phrases in Genesis 8 deliberately echo Genesis 1, marking the re-creation:

- 8:1: God "caused a ruach to pass over the earth" — echoing 1:2: "the ruach of God hovered over the face of the waters." In Genesis 1, the ruach preceded creation; in Genesis 8, the ruach precedes re-creation.
- 8:17: "be fruitful and multiply upon the earth" — the exact words of the creation blessing to the animals in Genesis 1:22: "be fruitful and multiply." The post-flood command repeats the creation mandate.
- 8:19: "every beast, every creeping thing, every bird — everything that moves upon the earth" — this enumeration echoes the creation categories of Genesis 1:20–25.

The formal translation preserves these echoes by maintaining lexical consistency with the vocabulary established in Genesis 1: the same words for "fruitful and multiply," "creeping things," "birds," and "all flesh" that were used in the creation and de-creation accounts are used here in the re-creation, so that the structural echo is visible to the attentive reader.

**3. The Altar and the Promise — The Covenant Foundation (vv. 20–22)**

The chapter's literary and theological climax is Noah's altar and the divine response. This passage performs several crucial functions:

First, it is the first explicit act of worship through constructed sacred space in the Bible. Cain and Abel brought offerings; Noah builds a structure — a mizbeach, a place of sacrifice — and burns burnt offerings on it. The altar is the origin of all subsequent Israelite altar theology.

Second, the divine response to the offering (vv. 21–22) is an unconditional unilateral promise — not yet the formal covenant of Genesis 9:8–17 (which is covenant with sign and stated conditions), but a divine resolve spoken "in His heart": "I will not again curse the ground... I will not again strike down every living thing... while the earth remains, [the natural order] shall not cease." This internal divine resolve is the theological foundation for everything that follows in Genesis 9 and beyond. The covenant of Genesis 9 formalizes and makes explicit what God has already resolved in 8:21.

Third, the diagnostic repetition of 6:5 in 8:21 — "the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth" — establishes that the basis of the divine promise is not human improvement but divine grace. God does not say "I have seen that Noah and his descendants are now better." God says the human being is still constitutionally inclined toward evil — and therefore God will not destroy the world again. The grace of 8:21 is the most radical grace in the flood narrative: it is given not despite the human problem but in full knowledge of the human problem and in conscious resolve not to let the human problem be grounds for cosmic destruction.

This is the theological center of the flood narrative's resolution: the world that exists after 8:21 is a world sustained by divine mercy in full awareness of human brokenness. The same diagnosis that opened the door to the flood closes it with a promise.

— LOGOS
